coredata.dc:title: Gold–silver nanocomposite-functionalized graphene based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor using graphene quantum dots coated porous PtPd nanochains as labels
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hongmei
Liu, Weiyan
Ma, Chao
Zhang, Yan
Wang, Xiu
Yu, Jinghua
Song, Xianrang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection of a tumor marker by using gold–silver nanocomposite-functionalized graphene as a sensing platform, which increased the surface area to capture a large number of primary antibodies as well as improving the electronic transmission rate. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were high quantum yield and high biocompatible which obtained by one-pot simple synthetic strategy. With property of good conductivity and large surface area, porous PtPd nanochains can conjugate more GQDs and second antibody to improve the sensitivity of the immunoassay. As a proof-of-concept, carbohydrate antigen 199 was used as a model analyte. Under optimal conditions, the ECL immunosensors exhibited a wide detection range (0.002–70 UmL−1) and a low detection limit (0.96 mUmL−1). Such immunosensor showed good precision, acceptable stability and reproducibility. In addition, the proposed method provided a new promising platform of clinical immunoassay for other biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
gold–silver nanocomposite-functionalized grapheme
porous PtPd nanochains

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanomaterial
Preparation of pPtPd@GQDs nanocomposites labeled anti-CA199
Fabrication of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor and detection
Characterization of GQDs, pPtPd nanochains and pPtPd@GQDs
Performance of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor for CA199 detection
Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the immunosensor
Application in analysis of serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Representative SEM image of graphene and the GN–Ag–Au nanomaterial (B), HRTEM image of the GN–Ag–Au nanomaterial (C), EDS of the GN–Ag–Au nanomaterial (D).

TEM images of GQDs (A), UV-vis and FL spectrum of the obtained GQDs solution (EX presents excitation spectra, EM presents emission spectra) (B). Inset: (upper) Emission spectra of the GQDs with excitation of different wavelength; (lower) the fluorescent photos by an UV beam of 365nm.

Value of ζ-potentials during the assembly process. The bars represent (a) pure GQDs, (b), porous PtPd nanochains and (c) pPtPd@GQDs (A), FT-IR spectra of GQDs (a), porous PtPd nanochains (b) and pPtPd@GQDs (c) (B). EIS spectra of bare GCE (a), GCE/GN–Ag–Au (b), GCE/GN–Ag–Au/Ab1(c), GCE/GN–Ag–Au/Ab1/BSA (d), the GCE/GN–Ag–Au/Ab1/BSA/CA 199 (e) and GCE/GN–Ag–Au/Ab1/BSA/CA199/GQDs@pPtPd (f) in a 5 mmol L [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe solution with 0.1M KCl (C). ECL profiles of the immuosensor using GN (a), GN-Ag (b) and GN-Ag-Au (c) modified GCE in pH 7.4 PBS containing TPA (D).

ECL of the immunosensor at various concentrations of CA199 (UmL−1): (a): 0.002, (b): 0.01, (c): 0.05, (d): 0.5, (e): 5, (f): 50 (A). Relationship between ECL and the CA199 concentration, each point was the average of ten measurements (B). The selectivity of the ECL immunosensor (C). ECL-potential curves of (a) pure GQDs, (b) pPtPd@GQD and cyclic voltammogram (inset) of pPtPd@GQD on the electrode in pH 7.4 PBS containing TPA.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of the immunosensor.
Assay results of clinical serum samples using the proposed and reference methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of ten successive determinations.

head.ce:title: Gold–silver nanocomposite-functionalized graphene based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor using graphene quantum dots coated porous PtPd nanochains as labels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Research Center, Shandong Tumor Hospital, Jinan 250012, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cancer Research Center, Shandong Tumor Hospital

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection of a tumor marker by using gold–silver nanocomposite-functionalized graphene as a sensing platform, which increased the surface area to capture a large number of primary antibodies as well as improving the electronic transmission rate. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were high quantum yield and high biocompatible which obtained by one-pot simple synthetic strategy. With property of good conductivity and large surface area, porous PtPd nanochains can conjugate more GQDs and second antibody to improve the sensitivity of the immunoassay. As a proof-of-concept, carbohydrate antigen 199 was used as a model analyte. Under optimal conditions, the ECL immunosensors exhibited a wide detection range (0.002–70 UmL−1) and a low detection limit (0.96 mUmL−1). Such immunosensor showed good precision, acceptable stability and reproducibility. In addition, the proposed method provided a new promising platform of clinical immunoassay for other biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: In clinical analysis, increased levels of tumor markers in human serum are associated with patients with cancer, one of the most threatening diseases for human beings [1]. Therefore, the determination of tumor markers in their early development stage plays an important role in screening for a disease. The immunoassay, based on the highly specific antibody–antigen recognition, has been widely used in the sensitive and accurate quantitative detection of tumor markers. Numerous immunoassay readout techniques such as colorimetric [2], chemiluminescence [3], electrochemical [4] and fluorescence [5] have been developed for the detection of tumor markers. However, some of them need expensive equipments and complex procedure, while the others suffer from low selectivity. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is initiated and controlled by the application of a suitable potential at an electrode surface allowing the time and position of the light-emitting reactions to be accurately controlled. By integrating the high affinity of antigen-antibody, ECL immunosensors has become a disposable, sensitive and selective analytical tool for determining clinical samples. However, the sensitivity of the ECL immunoassay is still low. Since the nanoparticles can work as a promoter to increase the surface area and improve the electron transfer at the electrode interface, various signal amplification technologies using nanomaterials have been developed.

Graphene, a monolayer of carbon atoms bonded as a dense honeycomb crystal structure has potential applications in sensors, integrated circuits, and new graphene devices. Due to their superior thermal properties [6], large specific surface area [7] and electronic peculiarities [8], it attracts a great deal of attention in materials science [9]. However, it is difficult for protein molecules to directly immobilize on the graphene [10]. Even if the biomolecules were physically adsorbed or covalently conjugated onto the graphene, the amount is still limited [11]. Hence, searching for a new graphene-based sensing platform with sensitive improvement is very necessary. Recently, a new class of hybrid materials, graphene supported noble nanocomposites which combining the advantages of both graphene substrate and active metal nanoparticle components could provide larger surface areas for the adsorption of biomolecules and effectively accelerate the electron transfer between electrode and detection molecules. It has shown extensive applications in many advanced fields optoelectronic transistors [12], catalysis [13], energy storage [14]. The noble metal (e.g., Au and Ag) with well-defined, low-cost and controlled shape have attracted increasing attention in metal based nanomaterials and become a kind of promising materials in ultrasensitive chemical and biological molecules detections. In this study, we tried to fabricate gold and silver nanoparticles-doped graphene hybrid nanomaterials (GN–Ag–Au) for the preparation of the ECL immunosensor. Due to the unique physical and chemical properties of Au and Ag particles [15], we anticipate that the carried hybrid nanomaterials could enhance the sensitivity of the sensor.

Except the superlative electronic properties, recent studies have demonstrated the luminescent characteristics of graphene, which can expand its application area in optical-related fields [16]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) sing star in carbon family, have recently been subjected to extensive research in materials science and engineering. Distinct from the “classic” luminescent nanoparticles (NPs) (e.g., qantum dots, dye-doped NPs and rare earth-based NPs) as well as silicon quantum dot, the GQDs not only exhibit interesting size- and wavelength-dependent down-conversion and up-conversion photoluminescence (PL), but also inherit the superior performance from graphene, such as large specific surface area, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent electrical conductivity and good thermal conductivity [17,18]. To the best of our knowledge, the preparation and PL mechanisms from the quantum size effect long with different emissive traps have been widely studied experimentally and theoretically over the last several years, the significant applied potentials of GQDs have just started to emerge. In this work, we prepared water-soluble GQDs by pyrolyzing citric acid which is fast and eco-friendly. The quantum yield of prepared GQDs is high because the GQDs aqueous solution shows bright blue emission under excitation of 365nm UV light in neutral pH. Based on the bright blue emission of GQDs, we develop an ECL immunosensor for the detection of carbohydrate antigen 199 (CA199).

But the ECL of pure GQDs is weak, nanoparticles can also be used as carriers to load a large amount of GQDs and biomolecules and thus afford the enhancement of performances of the biosensors. More significantly, it has been reported that L-cysteine (L-cys) and PtPd nanoparticles have a great enhancement to the ECL intensity, respectively [19,20]. Therefore, we deduced that the self-assembled multilayer of L-cys and PtPd could further greatly enhance the ECL signal because the successful formation of porous PtPd nanochains (pPtPd). It possess higher volume-to-surface ratio than that of single nanoparticle, which can conjugate more signal molecule and biomolecule to improve the sensitivity of the immunoassay.

In this work, a novel sandwich-type ECL immunesensor based on a GN–Ag–Au as sensor platform and GQDs functionalized pPtPd nanochains for signal amplification was designed. The GN–Ag–Au was performed by chemical reduction method and was used to modify the electrode, increasing the surface area to capture a large amount of primary antibodies as well as improving the electronic transmission rate. GQDs were biocompatibility, low toxicity and excellent electrical conductivity, which obtained through green synthesis route. In order to amplify the ECL responses, pPtPd nanochains were used as labels because of the large surface and conductivity. The CA199 was used as a model analyte, and the proposed immunosensor exhibited an excellent precision and sensitivity. Significantly, the new method may be quite promising, with potential broad applications in clinical immunoassays.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade or were the highest purity available and used directly for the following experiments without further purification and the aqueous solutions, unless indicated otherwise, were prepared with ultra-pure water which was obtained from a Lichun water purification system (18MVcm, Jinan, China). Carbohydrate Atigen (CA199), the primary anti-CA199 (Ab1) and the secondary anti-CA199 (Ab2) were gotten from Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96-99%) was obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). N-(3-dimethylamminopropyl)-N’- ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid, Chloroauric acid hydrated (HAuCl4 .4H2O), AgNO3, poly (N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidone) (PVP), NaBH4, ammonium hydroxide (25 wt%) and hydrazine (≥80 wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Re. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloropalladite (Na2PdCl4) and Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). L-cys was purchased from Kangda Amino Acid company (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS, pH 7.4) were prepared using 0.01mol L−1 KH2PO4 and 0.01mol L−1 Na2HPO4. The CA199 was stored at 4°C, and its standard solution was prepared daily with PBS solution as in use.

The ECL experiments were carried out on a MPI-B multipleparameter chemiluminescence analytical testing system equipped with a MPI-A/B multifunction chemiluminescence detector (Xi’An Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd. Xi’an, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy Sciences, China) at room temperature. UV-vis spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI Co., USA), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) data were obtained with an X-MAX50 X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (Oxford Co., UK), operated at 15kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on an electronic microscope (JEM 2100). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on an IM6x electrochemical station (Zahner, Germany). Infrared spectroscopy (IR) were obtained on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) Spectrum RX (PerkinElmer Spectoment). All experiments were carried out with a conventional three-electrode system with the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) as the working electrode (WE), a platinum counter electrode (CE) and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) reference electrode (RE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GN-Ag-Au hybrid nanomaterials mainly consisted of the following three steps, as shown in Scheme 1 . Prior to experiments, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [21]. Graphene nanosheets were synthesized by using a typical chemical reduction method toward graphene oxide. In brief, 5.0mg of GO was solubilized into 20mL of water by sonicating for 30min. Subsequently, 85mg of PVP was added into the above solution, and continuously stirred for 8h. Following that, 10μL of hydrazine solution (80 wt%) and 100μL of ammonia solution (25 wt%) were dissolved into the resulting dispersion drop by drop. After being vigorously stirred for a few minutes, the mixture was stirred for 1h at 100°C. Then cooling to room temperature (RT), the stable black dispersion (PVP-coated graphene nanosheets) was centrifuged and dispersed in 5.0mL of water.

The next step was to synthesize the Ag-coated graphene nanosheets. 200μL freshly prepared AgNO3 (75mM) was added into 5.0mL PVP-coated graphene solution obtained above, and the mixture was allowed to react at RT for 1h under vigorous stirring. During this procedure, the Ag+ was attached on the PVP-coated graphene nanosheets by using negatively charged NO3 − ions as bridge [22]. Soon afterwards, 100μL of freshly prepared NaBH4 (50mM) was added into the mixture and continuously stirred for 1h. During this process, the Ag+ was reduced to the zerovalent Ag nanoparticles.

The third step was to synthesize the Au on the Ag-functionalized PVP-coated graphene nanosheets by the excessive NaBH4 reduction of Au (III). In detail, 50μL of HAuCl4 (5 wt%) was slowly added to the mixture obtained above drop by drop then stirred for 2h at RT, until the color of the solution was dark brown. During this process, Au NPs were formed on the surface of graphene nanosheets due to the interaction between positively charged PVP and negatively charged AuCl4 − ions [23]. The obtained GN–Ag–Au nanomaterials was centrifuged and redispersed into 2.0mL ultrapure water, and used for the construction of the ECL immunosensor.

The GQDs were synthesized via pyrolyzing citric acid. Briefly, 1.0g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 280°C by oven for about 20min, then the 100mgmL−1 NaOH solution was added dropwise into the liquid under continuous stirring, so the pH was adjusted to 7.0. The GQDs were stored in the refrigerator (4°C) when not in use.

The pPtPd nanochains were synthetized as follows. At first, The PtPd was synthesized according to the literature. Briefly, 8.0mg CoCl2
. 6H2O and 110mg PVP were dissolved fully in 50mL sonicated ultrapure water by stirring for 10min. Subsequently, 10mL freshly prepared NaBH4 (35mM) was added dropwise into the solution and stired for 30min. Following that, 1.0mL H2PtCl6 (1%) and 5.0mL Na2PdCl4 (1%) were added into the mixture drop and drop with vigorous stirring. The blackish mixture was treated at RT for 30min under stirring. The whole synthesis procedure was under N2 protection. Finally, the black dispersion was collected by centrifugation and washed several times using ultrapure water. The PtPd synthesized above were added into L-cys (pH = 2.3) solution and stirred for 10h. During this procedure, L-cys and PtPd could interact with each other to form pPtPd nanochains due to the special covalent effect. The nanochains were collected by centrifugation and were redispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) to a volume of 1.0mL.

The preparation of pPtPd@GQDs was as follows, the pPtPd nanochains were mixed with 1.0mL of the prepared GQDs and 1.0mL of EDC (20mgmL−1). Then, the mixed suspension was stirred at 4°C for 4h. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with water for four times to remove unbound GQDs. Finally, the obtained pPtPd@GQDs nanocomposites were dispersed in PBS with a final volume of 1.0mL for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pPtPd@GQDs nanocomposites labeled anti-CA199
ce:section.ce:para: To generate the pPtPd@GQDs immunological labels, 1.0mL of the above pPtPd@GQDs suspension was mixed with 5.0mL of freshly prepared EDC (20mgmL−1) and stirred for 20min. Subsequently, 3.0mL of Ab2 solution (anti-CA199, 20μg·mL−1, in 0.01M pH 7.4 PBS) was added. After incubation at RT for 2h, the residual antibody was removed by centrifugation and the remaining material was washed with 0.01M PBS several times to obtain the Ab2 modified pPtPd@GQDs nanocomposites (pPtPd@GQDs/Ab2). The resultant pPtPd@GQDs/Ab2 was dispersed with PBS to a final volume of 2.0mL and stored at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor and detection
ce:section.ce:para: The whole process for constructing the immunosensor was illustrated in Scheme 1, (i) prior to use, GCE electrode with 3mm diameter was polished carefully with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05mm alumina powder on fine abrasive paper and washed ultrasonically with water. (ii) 5.0μL of the GN–Ag–Au was deposited on the electrode surface. After being dried under ambient conditions, it was washed and dropped 5.0μL of Ab1 (10μg·mL−1, in 0.01M pH 7.4 PBS) and reacted at RT for 50min. Subsequently, excess antibodies were washed off with PBS. Then 1% BSA solution was dropped and incubated for 1h to block nonspecific binding. Next, it was incubated with 5.0μL of CA199 standard solution, of different concentrations for 50min at RT, followed by washing with PBS. (iii) Finally, the immunosensor was further incubated with the prepared pPtPd@GQDs/Ab2 for 45min at RT then rinsed thoroughly with PBS to remove the unbound secondary antibodies. The ECL signal was detected in 0.01M pH 7.4 PBS containing 5.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 TPA which triggered under the cyclic voltammetry from 0.8 to 1.4V and the data was recorded on a MPI-E multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system at room temperature. The photomultiplier tube voltage was 800V. The ECL signals related to the CA199 concentrations could be measured.

To acquire an optimal output signal, the immobilization of biomolecules was crucial. Graphene nanosheet possess many unique features such as high electrical conductivity, high surface-to-volume ratio, high electron transfer rate that make it a promising host for immunosensor. Moreover, the extinction coefficient of the surface plasmon band for a silver particle was approximately four times as large as that for a gold particle of the same size. Hence, a major advance would be to devise a method for designing particles with the physical properties of silver nanoparticles but with the surface chemistry of gold nanoparticles. In this contribution, we combined the merits of Au NPs with Ag NPs to synthesize a GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanomaterial for the fabrication of ECL immunosensors.

To verify the successful synthesis of the GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanosheets, various characterization methods including SEM, TEM and EDS were employed. Fig. 1B showed a typical SEM image of the synthesized GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanosheets. Compared with pure graphene oxide (Fig. 1A), A large number of nanoparticles were observed on the surface of graphene nanosheet. A typical TEM image (Fig. 1C) of the as-prepared material contained two kinds of nanoparticles on graphene, well-defined spherical Au NPs (20nm) and smaller nanosized Ag NPs with an average size of 8.0nm. However, the size of these nanoparticles was not the same, and the dispersion was heterogeneous. The reason might be attributed to the stirring inhomogeneity of graphene nanosheets since graphene nanosheets was a substrate in the formation of Au and Ag nanoparticles on it. In fact, this was just an advantage over the scraggy surface for increasing the immobilized amount of biomolecules. In addition, biomolecules was assembled at the top and bottom of it.

EDS results for the GN–Ag–Au was used to investigate whether the nanoparticles were Au NPs and Ag NPs. As seen from Fig. 1D, the presence of amounts of C, Ag and Au in the sample were proved the successful synthesis of the GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, pPtPd nanochains and pPtPd@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2A, TEM image suggested that the size of the GQDs had a uniform shape and was narrowly distributed with diameters in the range of 5∼6nm. Fluorescence spectrum and UV-vis absorption spectra were studied to further explore the optical properties of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength at 351 and 453nm can be observed, also the emission wavelength of GQDs was nearly excitation-independent which could be ascribed to the transition from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) [24]. The excitation-independent emission of the GQDs implied that both the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQDs should be uniform. Moreover, the wavelength of the absorption peak of GQDs in UV-vis absorption spectra was almost the same as that of the excitation peak of GQDs. The GQDs aqueous solutions exhibited blue fluorescence excited by 365nm lamp (inset).

pPtPd nanochains with large specific surface area were employed as the carriers for GQDs immobilization, which would influence the loading of the secondary antibodies and the sensitivity performance of the resulting immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 2C, the obtained pPtPd nanochains were dispersive with an average diameter of about 70nm and the surface seem to be rough. The result confirmed the fact that the pPtPd nanochains were made up of PtPd with the help of L-cys.

The ζ-potential of pure GQDs (a), pPtPd nanochains (b) and pPtPd@GQDs (c) were shown in Fig. 3A, respectively. The GQDs were negatively charged with a ζ-potential of -10.3mV. Nevertheless, the ζ-potential of pPtPd nanochains was +15.1mV due to the amino groups. After the loading of negatively charged GQDs, the ζ-potential decreased to -17.2mV. The results were consistent with the successful fabrication of pPtPd@GQDs.

To further investigate the surface charge of the pPtPd nanochains in the fabrication process, the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, pPtPd nanochains and pPtPd@GQDs were shown in Fig. 3B. In the curve of GQDs, the presence of characteristic peaks around 1586cm−1 and 1399cm−1 were corresponded to the asymmetric stretching and asymmetric bending vibrations of COO- group which indicated that the surface of GQDs were full of carboxyl, which is in agreement with the literature [25]. In the curve of pPtPd nanochains, the peaks of 3169cm−1 and 2965cm−1 were attributed to asymmetric vibrations of N-H of the -NH3. Moreover, the bands 1538cm−1 and 1582cm−1 can be attributed to the symmetric bending mode of the NH3 group and COO- asymmetric mode, however, the characteristic peaks of S-H were not seen. These results demonstrated that the adsorption of L-cys molecules to the pPtPd surface through the formation of bond PtPd–S and the successful fabrication of pPtPd nanochains. After assemble the GQDs on the pPtPd nanochains, the corresponding absorption peaks of –NH2 become very weak or disappeared, because the -NH3 groups reacted with COO-. In addition, the characteristic peaks of COO- group around 1584cm−1 and 1399cm−1 were existed which were further used to immobilized Ab2.

EIS was an effective method for probing the features of surface-modified electrodes. The impedance spectra include a semicircle portion and a linear portion. The semicircle diameter at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron-transfer resistance (Ret), and the linear part at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion process. Fig. 3C showed the EIS of the immunosensors at different stages. Curve a showed the EIS of the bare GCE, where a semicircle with a large diameter was observed. When the GN–Ag–Au was immobilized on the surface of GCE (curve b), the diameter of this semicircle was reduced remarkably compared to the bare GCE, implying that the synthesized GN–Ag–Au was an excellent electric conductive material and accelerated the electron transfer. The observed improvements were attributed to a synergistic enhancement rather than merely the combination of the bietmallic Ag–Au nanoparticles and the graphene. The EIS changes during the stepwise modification process also demonstrated that the GN–Ag–Au layer was firmly immobilized on the surface of the GCE. When the Ab1 was conjugated on the GN–Ag–Au modified electrode, the Ret value was increased (curve c), which was mainly put down to the introduction of Ab1. Curve d denoted that the GCE was successively blocked by BSA. The result displayed the similar increase of the Ret value, which was consistent with the fact that the hydrophobic layer of the protein insulated the conductive support and perturbed the interfacial electron transfer. Further binding of CA199 retarded the interfacial electron transfer of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- pair, which provided evidence of the successful formation of the immunocomplex (curve e). It had been reported that the antigen–antibody complex could act as an inert block layer and disturb ion diffusion and change electrical capacitance, which both intensively affected the electrochemical impedance of electrodes during the formation of antigen–antibody complex [26]. This phenomenon was consistent with the greatly increased Ret value via the anchor of Ab2. In the final step, the attachment of pPtPd@GQDs/Ab2 bioconjugates gave rise to an additional barrier towards the access of redox probe to the electrode, which resulted in a further increment in the Ret (curve f). All the results suggested that the immobilized CA199 on the electrode could retain their native bioactivity and preliminarily apply for the determination of CA199. And these increases can entirely demonstrate the successful assembly processes on the immunosensor array.

To clarify the improved performance of the developed immunoassay using GN-Ag-Au as platform section, a comparative study of the ECL responses of the immunosensors was carried out using pPtPd@GQDs. We fabricated GN (curve a), GN-Ag (curve b) and GN-Ag-Au (curve c) immunosensors which were used for the detection of CA199 by using the sandwich-type immunoassay as shown in Fig. 3D. The immunosensor by using GN-Ag-Au modified GCE exhibited higher sensitivity than those of others. The presence of the GN-Ag-Au nanosheets with high surface areas and high conductivity not only enhanced the immobilized amount of biomolecules, but also effectively improved shuttle electrons rate from the base electrode surface to the bio-labels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the sandwich-type ECL immunosensor for CA199 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions (see in supporting information), the calibration curve for the determination of CA199 was shown in Fig. 4A. As can be seen, The ECL intensity of the immunosensor increased with increasing concentration of CA199. The calibration plots between the ECL intensity and the logarithm concentration of CA199 displayed a good linear relationships in the range 0.002 to 70 UmL−1, with a regression equation of ΔECL = 4185.3 + 1461.6 lg(cCA199, U·mL−1) and the correlation coefficient was 0.998 (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection for CA199 was 0.96 mUmL−1. Compared with previous studies [27], a wider linear range and lower detection limit were achieved with more simple steps for the immunosensor fabrication. The results demonstrated that the pPtPd@GQDs composite as an ECL label with the amplification technique greatly increased the sensitivity and could be used for the determination of CA199. Moreover, the GN-Ag-Au with large specific surface area generated more contact area to Ab1, moreover, the excellent electrical conductivity of GN-Ag-Au and pPtPd was a key element.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and reproducibility of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4C showed the specificity of the immunosensor, when 50 ngmL−1 human serum albumin (HSA), alpha fetoprotein (AFP), and prostate protein antigen (PSA) injected into the immunosensor respectively, no apparent change in the ECL intensity was observed compared with that of the blank test. Compared the ECL intensity of the mixture of the CA199 and the above interfering substance with the immunosensor in pure CA199, no significant difference was observed, indicating that the immunosensor display good specificity for the determination of CA199.

The precision and reproducibility of the proposed ECL immunoassay was evaluated by intra- and inter-assay relative standard deviation (RSD). The intra-assay precision of the analytical method was evaluated by analyzing three concentration levels, five times per run. It could be gained from experimental results that the RSD values of the intra-assay were 3.8, 5.2 and 4.3% for 0.01, 0.5, and 5 ng mL−1 CA199, respectively, whereas the RSD values of the inter-assay using different batches under the same conditions were 2.9%, 3.2%, and 4.1%. Thus, the precision and reproducibility of the immunosensors were acceptable.

When the immunosensors were not in use, they were stored in a refrigerator at 4°C, they were measured at intervals of 10 days, ECL intensity for the detection of 5 UmL−1 CA 199 was 95.7% of the initial response after a storage period of 50 days. In addition, the long-term stability of Ab2/GQDs@pPtPd was tested. When they were stored for 50 days, it remained 96.2% of initial responses which could attribute to the biocompatibility of GQDs and the strong interactions between Ab2 and GQDs@pPtPd. This result showed that not only the immunosensor possessed perfect stability, but the Ab2/GQDs@pPtPd bio-labels were stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in analysis of serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the immunoassay for clinical applications was investigated by analyzing several real samples (provided by the Shandong Cancer Hospital) in comparison with the reference value btained by a commercially available Electrochemiluminescent Analyzer (ROCHE E601, Switzerland) [28]. When the levels of the tumor markers were above the calibration range, serum samples were appropriately diluted with 0.01M PBS (pH 7.4) prior to the assay. The experimental results are summarized in Table 1 . The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2.78–7.02%. No significant differences were encountered, which demonstrated that the immunosensor was a promising tool for clinical determination of CA199 levels.

Fig. 4D showed the ECL-potential curves of the pure GQDs (curve a) and the pPtPd@GQDs (curve b) by cycling the potential between 0.8 and 1.4V, curve c was the cyclic voltammogram (CV) of the pPtPd@GQDs on the electrode (insert of Fig. 4D). One ECL peak appeared at 1.25V in both ECL curves in the anodic process, resulting from the reaction of GQDs with TPA, In comparison with the ECL of GQDs with and without pPtPd nanochains, the ECL intensity of pPtPd@GQDs was greatly enhanced. This could be explained by the fact that a larger number of GQDs were assembled on the pPtPd nanochains which could amplify the ECL signal. In the CV, an anodic peak was observed at +1.25V in pH 7.4 PBS containing TPA with an onset potential of +1.0V. We can get a conclusion that onset potential of +1.0V was attributed to the electro-oxidation of coreactant TPA. The peak +1.25V was assigned to reaction of GQDs with TPA which in accordance with ECL peak

The possible ECL mechanisms are as follows
(1) GQD–e→GQD•+
(2) TPA–e→TPA•+
(3) TPA•+−H+ →TPA•
(4) GQD•+ +TPA•+ →GQD* +TPA oxidant
(5) GQD* →GQD+hv

In conclusion, an ultrasensitive ECL immunoassay was developed by using water-soluble GQDs as ECL labels with the advantages of their low cytotoxicity, low cost, excellent solubility, ease of labeling and high biocompatibility. The possible response mechanism for the anodic ECL was proposed in the presence of coreactant TPA. Moreover, the signal could be further amplified by using pPtPd–labeled secondary antibodies. The GN–Ag–Au hybrid nanomaterial was used to modify the electrode, which increased the surface area to capture a large number of primary antibodies and improving the electronic transmission rate. Meanwhile, the proposed ECL mmunosensor exhibited good precision, acceptable stability and reproducibility, and could be used for the detection of CA199 in real serum samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Research Foundation of China (21277058, 21175058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2014.01.014.
